Adversarially review implemented task T119 (goal G7, defect D36). Return the structured verdict JSON {taskId, verdict:"approve"|"disapprove", criticism:[], questions:[], defects:[], rationale, summary} and WRITE NOTHING to the ledger.

Fetch acceptance: `mcp__ledger__fetch_item` ledger=tasks item=T119.
Branch implement/T119 at commit 9d5d0df3b8ef4501ed6cb75c59491c2bd02ab2de, based at main 180ac3e. Worktree: /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-ae276fef9602acfea.
Diff: `git -C /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia diff 180ac3e 9d5d0df3b8ef4501ed6cb75c59491c2bd02ab2de`.
Worker summary: dispatchInbound non-echo Probe branch consumes T116 epochChanged; on authenticated restart, Loads+nil-checks pr.resequencer and calls the NEW Resequencer.RebaselineToLow() outside m.mu. reseq low-anchor variant (pendingLow/pendingLowAnchor) keeps next pinned at pre-rebaseline release point, re-anchors only on a frame >1 window BELOW it; stale HIGH-seq straggler SUSPECT-dropped (closes D36 re-pin race); idempotent; unstarted-ring no-op; seq<anchor underflow guard. Tests drive real reflector restart handshake through handleInbound→dispatchInbound for primary + AddConcentratorPeer; verified fails without wiring AND with plain Rebaseline. Touched multipath.go, reseq.go, peer_restart_rebaseline_test.go, reseq_test.go, design.md.

Independently scrutinize (frontier datapath change): (1) lock discipline — RebaselineToLow outside m.mu, resequencer mutex never nested (no deadlock/lock-order inversion); (2) low-anchor correctness — stale HIGH straggler cannot re-pin next high, low-seq init admits; (3) unsigned-underflow guard (seq<anchor) + the 'more than one window below' threshold off-by-one; (4) fires only on genuine restart, covers primary + concentrator per-peer resequencers both directions, same-epoch no re-anchor, other peer undisturbed; (5) D32 hub-failover path + test untouched; (6) tests genuinely discriminate (fail without fix AND with plain Rebaseline). Run `cd /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-ae276fef9602acfea && nix develop -c sh -c 'go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./internal/bind/... ./internal/reseq/...' && nix develop -c sh -c 'go test -race ./internal/bind/...'`. Split criticism[] vs questions[]; file genuine defects. Be maximally adversarial on the low-anchor window arithmetic and lock nesting.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

Atomically Load the resequencer and nil-check it (absent mid-teardown, like the DATA branch; a torn-down peer re-instantiates an UNSTARTED ring that needs no rebaseline). Perform the re-anchor OUTSIDE m.mu with no other lock held — the resequencer keeps its own mutex; NEVER nest it — mirroring the SetPeerRemote→Rebaseline discipline (multipath.go ~:2167-2183). Because `pr` is the demux-resolved per-peer view, this SINGLE site covers BOTH the edge single-concentrator primary resequencer AND every concentrator per-peer resequencer, and both restart directions. content.content:
185	}
186	
187	// Observe ingests one DATA frame's inner payload under its outer-seq. It takes
188	// ownership of payload (the caller must not mutate it afterwards); the multipath
189	// Bind passes the freshly-decoded frame.Data.Payload, which aliases nothing else.
190	// src is the outer source address the frame arrived from. Any frames that become
191	// deliverable are appended to the ready FIFO for Pop.
192	func (r *Resequencer) Observe(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) {
193		now := r.clock.Now()
194		r.mu.Lock()
195		defer r.mu.Unlock()
196	
197		if !r.started {
198			r.started = true
199			r.next = seq
200		}
201	
202		// Give up on any head-of-line gap whose hold time has elapsed before deciding
203		// where this frame lands.
204		r.expire(now)
205	
206		// Classify the frame against the release window and the discontinuity guard.
207		// admit adjusts next (window-advance or resync) when warranted and reports
208		// whether the frame now lands inside [next, next+window) and should be placed.
209		if !r.admit(seq) {
210			return
211		}
212	
213		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
214		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
215			r.dropDup++
216			return
217		}
218		cell.seq = seq
219		cell.src = src
220		cell.payload = payload
221		cell.occupied = true
222		r.buf++
223		if r.buf > r.highWater {
224			r.highWater = r.buf
225		}
226	
227		r.drain()
228		r.arm(now)
229	}
230	
231	// ObserveRecovered ingests a payload the FEC decoder reconstructed from parity (T24).
232	// Unlike Observe it NEVER moves or re-pins the release point: a recovered frame is a
233	// REPAIR for a past gap, so if it has already fallen below the release point it is
234	// dropped as too-late, and it can neither window-advance nor corroborate a resync — so
235	// FEC recovery can never CAUSE the burst loss it exists to prevent (a late batch of K
236	// recovered seqs below `next` must not be able to dump the live buffer via a backward
237	// resync). It returns whether the frame was PLACED (landed at or above the release
238	// point and will be delivered in order); a dropped-late recovered frame returns false,
239	// which the datapath uses to keep the /metrics recovered counter honest — counting
240	// frames actually delivered ahead of the release point, not merely reconstructed.
241	func (r *Resequencer) ObserveRecovered(seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) bool {
242		now := r.clock.Now()
243		r.mu.Lock()
244		defer r.mu.Unlock()
245	
246		if !r.started {
247			r.started = true
248			r.next = seq
249		}
250		r.expire(now)
251	
252		// The placement window is EXACTLY [next, next+window). A recovered frame below next
253		// is too late (drop, and — crucially — never feed the resync guard). One at or beyond
254		// next+window would require a window-advance we refuse to perform for a reconstructed
255		// frame, since that would skip live seqs on the strength of a repair. Neither branch
256		// touches next or the corroboration run.
257		if seq < r.next {
258			if r.next-seq > r.window {
259				r.dropSuspect++
260			} else {
261				r.dropLate++
262			}
263			return false
264		}
265		if seq-r.next >= r.window {
266			r.dropLate++
267			return false
268		}
269	
270		cell := &r.ring[seq%r.window]
271		if cell.occupied && cell.seq == seq {
272			r.dropDup++
273			return false // already buffered (a received frame or a prior recovery)
274		}
275		cell.seq = seq
276		cell.src = src
277		cell.payload = payload
278		cell.occupied = true
279		r.buf++
280		if r.buf > r.highWater {
281			r.highWater = r.buf
282		}
283	
284		r.drain()
285		r.arm(now)
286		return true
287	}
288	
289	// admit classifies seq against the release window and the discontinuity guard and
290	// decides whether the frame should be buffered. It returns true when seq now lies
291	// inside [next, next+window) and should be placed; false (having bumped the
292	// appropriate drop counter) when the frame is dropped. It may advance next by a

1	package bind

11	)
12	
13	// The two sessionIDs a "restart" spans: the pre-restart boot and the post-restart boot.
14	// Distinct values make the second adoption an epoch change (a new-sessionID adoption over
15	// an already-adopted path), which is exactly what the reflector reports as epochChanged.
16	const (
17		preRestartSession  uint64 = 0xAAAA0000AAAA0001
18		postRestartSession uint64 = 0xBBBB0000BBBB0002
19		// restartHighSeq is a release point far past resequencerWindow (2048): the pre-restart
20		// boot's busy stream advanced `next` here, so the restarted boot's low outer-seq init is
21		// >1 window below it — the SUSPECT region that, before T119, blackholed the wrapped init.
22		restartHighSeq uint64 = 9000
23	)
24	
25	// deliverDATA encodes one DATA frame under psk and pushes it through the FULL receive path
26	// (handleInbound → dispatchInbound's DATA branch → the peer's resequencer), exactly as a
27	// wire datagram would arrive on view. It exercises the per-peer routing rather than poking
28	// the resequencer directly, so the assertions read the resequencer the wiring actually feeds.
29	func deliverDATA(t testing.TB, m *Multipath, view *peerPathState, psk config.Key, seq uint64, payload []byte, src netip.AddrPort) {
30		t.Helper()
31		codec, err := frame.NewCodec(psk)
32		if err != nil {
33			t.Fatalf("build data codec: %v", err)
34		}
35		raw, err := codec.Encode(nil, frame.Data{OuterSeq: seq, PathID: view.id, Payload: payload})
36		if err != nil {
37			t.Fatalf("encode data seq %d: %v", seq, err)
38		}
39		m.handleInbound(view, raw, src)
40	}
41	
42	// reflectProbeIssuedChallenge drives ONE inbound PROBE (encoded under psk) through
43	// handleInbound → dispatchInbound's non-echo Probe branch — the reflector adopt/restart
44	// path plus the T119 epochChanged→RebaselineToLow wiring — and returns the issued challenge
45	// carried in the reflected echo (read off the raw peer socket). Sending the reflector's own
46	// live challenge back on the NEXT probe is what makes it adopt (the responder-contributed-
47	// challenge protocol), so a caller learns the challenge here and echoes it next.
48	func reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t testing.TB, m *Multipath, view *peerPathState, psk config.Key, peer *net.UDPConn, peerAP netip.AddrPort, sessionID, probeSeq, echoedChallenge uint64) uint64 {
49		t.Helper()
50		raw, err := frame.Encode(psk, frame.Probe{
51			PathID:         view.id,
52			ProbeSeq:       probeSeq,
53			TimestampNanos: time.Now().UnixNano(),
54			SessionID:      sessionID,
55			Challenge:      echoedChallenge,
56		})
57		if err != nil {
58			t.Fatalf("encode probe (session %#x seq %d): %v", sessionID, probeSeq, err)
59		}
60		m.handleInbound(view, raw, peerAP)
61		codec, err := frame.NewCodec(psk)
62		if err != nil {
63			t.Fatalf("build echo codec: %v", err)
64		}
65		echo := readProbe(t, peer, codec)
66		return echo.Challenge
67	}
68	
69	// runPeerRestartRebaselineScenario is the shared body of the T119 acceptance. It drives one
70	// peer through: bootstrap adoption (no re-baseline), a busy stream advancing `next` far past
71	// one window, an authenticated RESTART (new-sessionID adopt → epochChanged=true → the wiring
72	// low-anchor re-baselines THIS peer's resequencer), a STALE-HIGH old-boot straggler that must
73	// NOT re-pin `next` high, the wrapped LOW-seq init that must then admit, and a same-epoch probe
74	// that must NOT re-anchor — all while a second bound peer (witnessPeer) is left untouched.
75	//
76	// It documents the failing-without-the-wiring contract: with the epochChanged→RebaselineToLow
77	// call removed, the restart leaves `next` pinned high, Rebaselines stays 0, and the wrapped
78	// low-seq init is SUSPECT-dropped — the delivery assertion below fails (the D36 repro).
79	func runPeerRestartRebaselineScenario(
80		t *testing.T,
81		m *Multipath,
82		targetView *peerPathState, targetPeer *peerState, targetPSK config.Key,
83		witnessView *peerPathState, witnessPeer *peerState, witnessPSK config.Key,
84	) {
85		t.Helper()
86		peer, peerAP := rawPeer(t)
87	
88		rq := targetPeer.resequencer.Load()
89		wrq := witnessPeer.resequencer.Load()
90		if rq == nil || wrq == nil {
91			t.Fatalf("resequencer not instantiated: target=%v witness=%v", rq != nil, wrq != nil)
92		}
93	
94		dataSrc := netip.MustParseAddrPort("203.0.113.9:51820")
95		lowSrc := netip.MustParseAddrPort("198.51.100.4:51820")
96	
97		// --- Bootstrap adoption (session preRestartSession): learn challenge, then adopt. A
98		//     first-ever adoption is NOT a restart, so it must NOT re-baseline. ---
99		c := reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, peer, peerAP, preRestartSession, 0, 0)
100		_ = reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, peer, peerAP, preRestartSession, 1, c)
101		if got := rq.Stats().Rebaselines; got != 0 {
102			t.Fatalf("bootstrap adoption re-baselined the resequencer: Rebaselines=%d, want 0", got)
103		}
104	
105		// --- The pre-restart boot's busy stream advances BOTH peers' release point far past one
106		//     window (via the real DATA receive path). ---

109			t.Fatalf("high boot seq %d not delivered — release point did not advance", restartHighSeq)
110		}
111		deliverDATA(t, m, witnessView, witnessPSK, restartHighSeq, []byte("witness-high"), dataSrc)

114		}
115	
116		// --- Authenticated PEER RESTART (session postRestartSession): learn the live challenge,
117		//     then adopt with it. This adoption is over an ALREADY-adopted path under a DIFFERENT
118		//     session → epochChanged=true → the T119 wiring low-anchor re-baselines THIS peer. ---
119		c2 := reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, peer, peerAP, postRestartSession, 0, 0)
120		_ = reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, peer, peerAP, postRestartSession, 1, c2)
121		if got := rq.Stats().Rebaselines; got != 1 {
122			t.Fatalf("peer restart did NOT re-baseline the target resequencer: Rebaselines=%d, want 1 "+
123				"(the epochChanged→RebaselineToLow wiring is the code under test)", got)
124		}
125	
126		// --- STALE-HIGH RACE: a stale OLD-boot high-seq straggler still draining from carrier/modem
127		//     queues lands BEFORE the low init. It must be SUSPECT-dropped and must NOT re-pin `next`
128		//     high (which a plain unpin-and-trust-next re-baseline would allow, blocking recovery). ---
129		beforeSuspect := rq.Stats().DroppedSuspect
130		deliverDATA(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, restartHighSeq+50, []byte("stale-high-straggler"), dataSrc)
131		if it, ok := rq.Pop(); ok {
132			t.Fatalf("stale-high straggler was DELIVERED (%q) — it re-pinned next high (D36 race not closed)", it.Payload)
133		}
134		if rq.Stats().DroppedSuspect <= beforeSuspect {
135			t.Fatalf("stale-high straggler not SUSPECT-dropped: DroppedSuspect stayed %d", beforeSuspect)
136		}
137	
138		// --- The wrapped WG init (restarted stream, outer-seq ~1) now admits: it is >1 window below
139		//     the pre-rebaseline release point, so it re-anchors and DELIVERS, and it must NOT itself
140		//     count as a suspect drop. ---
141		suspectBeforeLow := rq.Stats().DroppedSuspect
142		deliverDATA(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, 1, []byte("wrapped-wg-init"), lowSrc)
143		it, ok := rq.Pop()
144		if !ok || string(it.Payload) != "wrapped-wg-init" {
145			t.Fatalf("wrapped low-seq init NOT delivered after restart re-anchor: ok=%v payload=%q", ok, it.Payload)
146		}
147		if got := rq.Stats().DroppedSuspect; got != suspectBeforeLow {
148			t.Fatalf("the low-seq init was counted as a suspect drop: DroppedSuspect %d → %d", suspectBeforeLow, got)
149		}
150	
151		// --- A SAME-epoch (non-restart) probe must NOT re-anchor: a within-session probe reports
152		//     epochChanged=false, so the wiring leaves the release point alone. ---
153		rebBefore := rq.Stats().Rebaselines
154		_ = reflectProbeIssuedChallenge(t, m, targetView, targetPSK, peer, peerAP, postRestartSession, 2, 0)
155		if got := rq.Stats().Rebaselines; got != rebBefore {
156			t.Fatalf("a same-epoch (non-restart) probe re-baselined: Rebaselines %d → %d", rebBefore, got)
157		}
158	
159		// --- The other bound peer's resequencer is UNDISTURBED by the target's restart. ---
160		if got := wrq.Stats().Rebaselines; got != 0 {
161			t.Fatalf("witness peer re-baselined by the target's restart: Rebaselines=%d, want 0", got)
162		}
163		wSuspectBefore := wrq.Stats().DroppedSuspect
164		deliverDATA(t, m, witnessView, witnessPSK, 1, []byte("witness-should-drop"), lowSrc)
165		if it, ok := wrq.Pop(); ok {
166			t.Fatalf("witness delivered a low seq (%q) — its release point was disturbed by the target's restart", it.Payload)
167		}
168		if got := wrq.Stats().DroppedSuspect; got <= wSuspectBefore {
169			t.Fatalf("witness did not reject the low seq as suspect (its next moved): DroppedSuspect stayed %d", wSuspectBefore)

171	}

200	// newConcentratorPairForRestart builds an Open 2-peer concentrator over one shared socket: the
201	// primary keyed on pskA and a beta peer registered via AddConcentratorPeer keyed on pskB, with
202	// beta's heavy receive datapath (its resequencer) instantiated so a test can drive DATA at it
203	// directly rather than waiting for the demux to lazily bind its first PROBE.
204	func newConcentratorPairForRestart(t *testing.T, pskA, pskB config.Key) (*Multipath, *peerState) {

206		clk := newFakeClock()

212			t.Fatalf("AddConcentratorPeer: %v", err)
213		}
214		if _, _, err := m.Open(0); err != nil {
215			t.Fatalf("Open: %v", err)
216		}
217		t.Cleanup(func() { _ = m.Close() })
218	
219		beta := m.peersByName["beta"]
220		if beta == nil {

222		}
223		// A concentrator peer's resequencer is lazily built on its first bound PROBE; instantiate it
224		// eagerly here so the test drives its receive plane deterministically.
225		m.ensurePeerReceiveInstantiated(beta)

index 153d394..4e72111 100644
--- a/docs/design.md
+++ b/docs/design.md
@@ -409,6 +409,27 @@ WireGuard anti-replay window sees the traffic — critical, because WG would
 otherwise drop legitimately-reordered datagrams. It runs its **own outer sequence
 space** and never touches the inner WireGuard counter (a core invariant).
 
+**Trusted re-baseline on a peer restart (T116/T119).** A DATA frame is
+unauthenticated, so the release point is normally moved only by the corroborating
+`resync` guard (several distinct low seqs within one window) — which a *single*
+low frame cannot trip. That is fine for reorder, but a **peer/concentrator process
+restart** resets the sender's outer-seq near 1 far below the release point the prior
+boot's high-rate stream advanced `next` to, and the restarted peer's wrapped WG init
+is a *lone* low frame — it would be dropped as *suspect* and the tunnel would never
+re-establish. The restart is detected on the **authenticated** liveness plane: the
+per-peer probe reflector reports an `epochChanged` when a probe adopts an
+already-adopted path under a **new session id** (a genuine restart, deduped once per
+epoch), and `dispatchInbound` re-baselines *that* peer's resequencer via
+`Resequencer.RebaselineToLow`. Because this fires on the demux-resolved per-peer view,
+the one call site covers both the edge single-concentrator primary and every
+concentrator per-peer resequencer, in either restart direction. Unlike the hub-failover
+`Rebaseline` (which unpins and re-anchors on the *next* frame), the **low-anchor**
+variant re-anchors only on a frame more than one window *below* the pre-rebaseline
+release point — so a stale HIGH-seq straggler still draining from the old boot's queues
+is suspect-dropped and cannot re-pin `next` high and block recovery (the D36 re-pin
+race). Both re-baselines are sound because a hub switch and an authenticated epoch change
+are **trusted control events**, not forgeable wire frames.
+
 ### FEC — `internal/fec` + `internal/adaptivefec`
 
 - `fec` implements Reed-Solomon over groups of *K* data shards + *M* parity

--- a/internal/reseq/reseq_test.go
+++ b/internal/reseq/reseq_test.go
@@ -798,3 +798,85 @@ func TestRebaselineDiscardsBufferedPreSwitchFrames(t *testing.T) {
 		t.Fatalf("released FIFO after Rebaseline = %v, want [0] (prior deliveries must be untouched)", got)
 	}
 }
+
+// TestRebaselineToLowSurvivesStaleHighStragglerRace is the reseq-level D36 repro (plan
+// review R126). Under the saturation precondition the plain Rebaseline (unpin + trust
+// the NEXT frame) loses a race: after a PEER RESTART re-baseline, a stale HIGH-seq
+// straggler still draining from the OLD boot can land BEFORE the restarted low-seq init
+// and re-pin `next` HIGH, and with once-per-epoch restart dedup recovery is then blocked.
+// RebaselineToLow re-anchors ONLY on a frame more than one window below the pre-rebaseline
+// release point, so the stale-high straggler is SUSPECT-dropped and the subsequent low
+// init still admits. The parallel with plain Rebaseline is deliberate: swap RebaselineToLow
+// for Rebaseline here and the straggler re-pins next high, the low init is dropped, and the
+// final delivery assertion fails.
+func TestRebaselineToLowSurvivesStaleHighStragglerRace(t *testing.T) {
+	clk := newFakeClock()
+	const window = 64
+	r := reseq.New(window, time.Second, clk)
+
+	// Prior boot: a busy contiguous stream advances the release point well past one
+	// window (next == priorHi afterwards).
+	const priorHi = 200
+	for s := uint64(0); s < priorHi; s++ {
+		r.Observe(s, payloadOf(s), testSrc)
+	}
+	_ = drain(r)
+
+	// Authenticated peer restart: the low-anchor re-baseline pins the pending anchor at
+	// the current (high) release point rather than unpinning outright.
+	r.RebaselineToLow()
+	if s := r.Stats(); s.Rebaselines != 1 {
+		t.Fatalf("Rebaselines = %d, want 1", s.Rebaselines)
+	}
+
+	// The re-pin RACE: a stale OLD-boot HIGH-seq straggler arrives FIRST. It must be
+	// SUSPECT-dropped and must NOT re-pin `next` high (which a plain Rebaseline would
+	// let it do, blocking recovery).
+	const staleHigh = priorHi + 5 // above the old release point: a genuine "high" straggler
+	beforeSuspect := r.Stats().DroppedSuspect
+	r.RebaselineToLow() // idempotent: a repeated restart signal keeps the original high anchor
+	if s := r.Stats(); s.Rebaselines != 2 {
+		t.Fatalf("Rebaselines = %d after a repeated restart signal, want 2", s.Rebaselines)
+	}
+	r.Observe(staleHigh, payloadOf(staleHigh), testSrc)
+	if got := drain(r); len(got) != 0 {
+		t.Fatalf("stale-high straggler was DELIVERED %v — it re-pinned next high (D36 race not closed)", got)
+	}
+	if r.Stats().DroppedSuspect <= beforeSuspect {
+		t.Fatal("stale-high straggler was not SUSPECT-dropped (DroppedSuspect did not increase)")
+	}
+
+	// Now the genuine restarted-stream low-seq init arrives: it is more than one window
+	// below the pre-rebaseline release point, so it re-anchors and DELIVERS, and it must
+	// NOT itself count as a suspect drop.
+	const lowInit = 1
+	suspectBeforeLow := r.Stats().DroppedSuspect
+	r.Observe(lowInit, payloadOf(lowInit), testSrc)
+	r.Observe(lowInit+1, payloadOf(lowInit+1), testSrc)
+	if got := drain(r); !equalSeqs(got, []uint64{lowInit, lowInit + 1}) {
+		t.Fatalf("after RebaselineToLow the restarted low stream delivered %v, want [%d %d]", got, lowInit, lowInit+1)
+	}
+	if r.Stats().DroppedSuspect != suspectBeforeLow {
+		t.Fatalf("the low-seq init was counted as a suspect drop: DroppedSuspect %d -> %d", suspectBeforeLow, r.Stats().DroppedSuspect)
+	}
+}
+
+// TestRebaselineToLowNoOpBeforeFirstObserve pins that a low-anchor re-baseline on an
+// UNSTARTED ring (a torn-down peer re-instantiated a fresh resequencer) enters NO pending
+// mode: the first Observe pins `next` normally, whatever its seq, exactly as a fresh ring
+// behaves. It bumps the diagnostic counter but must not blackhole the first stream.
+func TestRebaselineToLowNoOpBeforeFirstObserve(t *testing.T) {
+	clk := newFakeClock()
+	r := reseq.New(64, time.Second, clk)
+
+	r.RebaselineToLow()
+	if s := r.Stats(); s.Rebaselines != 1 {
+		t.Fatalf("Rebaselines = %d, want 1", s.Rebaselines)
+	}
+	// A fresh high-seq stream pins next and delivers in order — no pending-low gate.
+	r.Observe(5000, payloadOf(5000), testSrc)
+	r.Observe(5001, payloadOf(5001), testSrc)
+	if got := drain(r); !equalSeqs(got, []uint64{5000, 5001}) {
+		t.Fatalf("unstarted RebaselineToLow blackholed the first stream: delivered %v, want [5000 5001]", got)
+	}
+}

### Session summary
- **Did:** reviewed task T119 round 1 (diff 180ac3e..9d5d0df, worktree `/home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-ae276fef9602acfea`)
- **Achieved:** verdict disapprove; 2 criticisms / 0 questions
- **Discovered:** wiring, lock discipline, reflector dedup, low-anchor arithmetic for the saturated D36 case, both test files, doc sync, and all check commands (build/vet/full suite/`-race`) verified green; tests genuinely discriminate (plain `Rebaseline` delivers the stale-high straggler; missing wiring leaves `Rebaselines=0`); D32 test (`internal/bind/per_peer_reseq_test.go`) untouched and passing. However, two defects reproduced empirically with throwaway tests (written, run, removed): (1) `RebaselineToLow` with `next <= window` sets an unsatisfiable `pendingLow` predicate — 499/499 new-boot frames SUSPECT-dropped, permanent blackhole with no escape path (a regression: pre-T119 the resync corroboration recovered this); (2) plain `Rebaseline()` does not clear `pendingLow`, so a D32 hub failover/fail-back after a pending restart re-anchor delivered 0/100 frames, violating `Rebaseline`'s documented "next Observe re-anchors" postcondition.
- **Issues:** none

The implementation is substantially correct for the task's target scenario — the saturated-restart D36 repro, the stale-high re-pin race, per-peer scoping, and lock discipline all check out (verified `dispatchInbound` runs on readLoops with no `m.mu` held at either call site, and `RebaselineToLow` takes only `r.mu` with no calls under it). The two findings are boundary interactions of the new `pendingLow` state in `/home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-ae276fef9602acfea/internal/reseq/reseq.go`: the predicate `seq < anchor && anchor-seq > window` (reseq.go:305) is unsatisfiable for any seq when `anchor <= window`, and nothing else ever clears `pendingLow` (`tryResync` is unreachable behind the early return; repeated restart signals deliberately keep the original anchor; there is no timeout), while `Rebaseline()` (reseq.go:574) resets `started` but not `pendingLow`.

```json
{
  "taskId": "T119",
  "verdict": "disapprove",
  "criticism": [
    "Permanent datapath blackhole when the restart fires with next <= window: RebaselineToLow (reseq.go:602) unconditionally enters pendingLow with anchor=next, but the re-anchor predicate `seq < anchor && anchor-seq > window` (reseq.go:305) is unsatisfiable for ANY seq when anchor <= window, and nothing else ever clears pendingLow (tryResync is bypassed by the early return, repeated restart signals keep the original anchor, there is no timeout). Reproduced: window=64, old boot sent 50 frames, RebaselineToLow, then 499 new-boot frames — all 499 SUSPECT-dropped, zero delivered, forever. This is a regression: pre-T119 the same scenario recovered via resync corroboration within ~3 low frames. Realistic trigger: a crash-looping peer — after the first restart re-anchors next to ~1, a second restart within ~one window of traffic pins a small anchor. Fix: enter pendingLow only when next > window+1 (below that the stale-high and restarted-low seq ranges overlap within one window, so the D36 race is indistinguishable and plain unpin semantics suffice — set started=false instead), and add a regression test for the small-anchor restart.",
    "Plain Rebaseline() (reseq.go:574) does not clear pendingLow, violating its documented postcondition that the NEXT Observe re-anchors next: after RebaselineToLow (restart pending, low init not yet arrived) a D32 SetPeerRemote->Rebaseline hub failover leaves the stale pendingLow gate in force — the first frame re-pins next via !started, but every subsequent frame is re-classified against the STALE pendingLowAnchor. Reproduced: anchor=200/window=64, Rebaseline(), then a fail-back hub streaming seqs 300..399 — 0 of 100 delivered, all SUSPECT-dropped, permanently. Fix: clear pendingLow in Rebaseline() (a full unpin supersedes the pending low-anchor), plus a test covering the RebaselineToLow->Rebaseline interleaving."
  ],
  "questions": [],
  "defects": [],
  "rationale": "The saturated-restart case, wiring, lock discipline, and tests are sound and all checks pass, but two empirically reproduced interactions of the new pendingLow state cause permanent per-peer blackholes: an unsatisfiable re-anchor predicate when the restart anchor is <= one window (a regression vs the pre-T119 resync recovery), and plain Rebaseline() failing to clear pendingLow, breaking the D32 hub-failover contract.",
  "summary": "Disapprove: low-anchor mechanism correct for the D36 saturated case, but pendingLow permanently blackholes small-anchor restarts (crash-loop) and survives plain Rebaseline(), breaking the D32 re-anchor contract — both reproduced."
}
```
